([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 第18回オリンピック冬季競技大会, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Dai Jūhachi-kai Orinpikku Tōkikyōgi Taikai*) and commonly known as **Nagano 1998** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 長野1998), were a winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 7 to 22 February 1998, mainly in [Nagano Prefecture](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture"), Japan, with some events taking place in the nearby mountain communities of [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba "Hakuba"), [Karuizawa](/wiki/Karuizawa,_Nagano "Karuizawa, Nagano"), [Nozawa Onsen](/wiki/Nozawaonsen "Nozawaonsen"), and [Yamanouchi](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"). The city of Nagano had previously been a candidate to host the [1940 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1940_Winter_Olympics "1940 Winter Olympics") (which were later cancelled), as well as the [1972 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics"), but had been eliminated at the national level by [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo") on both occasions.

The **1998 Winter Olympics**, officially known as the **XVIII Olympic Winter Games** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 第18回オリンピック冬季競技大会, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Dai Jūhachi-kai Orinpikku Tōkikyōgi Taikai*) and commonly known as **Nagano 1998** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 長野1998), were a winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 7 to 22 February 1998, mainly in [Nagano Prefecture](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture"), Japan, with some events taking place in the nearby mountain communities of [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba "Hakuba"), [Karuizawa](/wiki/Karuizawa,_Nagano "Karuizawa, Nagano"), [Nozawa Onsen](/wiki/Nozawaonsen "Nozawaonsen"), and [Yamanouchi](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"). The city of Nagano had previously been a candidate to host the [1940 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1940_Winter_Olympics "1940 Winter Olympics") (which were later cancelled), as well as the [1972 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics"), but had been eliminated at the national level by [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo") on both occasions.

The games hosted 2,176 athletes from 72 nations competing in 7 sports and 68 events.[[1]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) The number of athletes and participating nations were a record at the time. The Games saw the introduction of [women's ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics"). [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") players were allowed to participate in the [men's ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics") for the first time. [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Azerbaijan at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Kenya at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Macedonia at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Uruguay at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Venezuela at the 1998 Winter Olympics") made their debut at the Winter Olympics.

The athlete who won the most medals at these games was the [Russian](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [cross-country skier](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)") [Larisa Lazutina](/wiki/Larisa_Lazutina "Larisa Lazutina") who won five medals, including three gold. The [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") cross-country skier [Bjørn Dæhlie](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie "Bjørn Dæhlie") won four medals, including three gold, which took his total Olympic medal total to 12, including eight gold, which is a record for Winter Olympics. [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1998 Winter Olympics") men's ice hockey team won the gold medal. In [Ski Jumping](/wiki/Ski_Jumping "Ski Jumping"), [Kazuyoshi Funaki](/wiki/Kazuyoshi_Funaki "Kazuyoshi Funaki") won two gold medals and one silver for host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics"). The [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Figure skater](/wiki/Figure_skater "Figure skater") [Tara Lipinski](/wiki/Tara_Lipinski "Tara Lipinski") became the youngest champion in Olympic history at the age of 15 years and 255 days. [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") dominated the medal table with 29 medals, including 12 gold. Germany was followed by Norway and Russia, who won 25 and 18 medals respectively. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), which finished fourth in the medal table with 15 medals, including six gold, had its most successful Winter Olympics up until that point.

The host was selected on 15 June 1991, over [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), [Östersund](/wiki/%C3%96stersund "Östersund"), [Jaca](/wiki/Jaca "Jaca") and [Aosta](/wiki/Aosta "Aosta"). They were the third Olympic Games and second Winter Olympics to be held in Japan, after the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") in Tokyo and the [1972 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics") in [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"). The games were succeeded by the [1998 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Paralympics "1998 Winter Paralympics") from 5 to 14 March. These were the final Winter Olympic Games under the IOC Presidency of [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch").

The hosting of the games improved transportation networks with the construction of the high-speed *[Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen")*, the Nagano Shinkansen (now the [Hokuriku Shinkansen](/wiki/Hokuriku_Shinkansen "Hokuriku Shinkansen")), between [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo_Station "Tokyo Station") and [Nagano Station](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station"), via [Ōmiya](/wiki/%C5%8Cmiya_Station_(Saitama) "Ōmiya Station (Saitama)") and [Takasaki](/wiki/Takasaki_Station "Takasaki Station"). In addition, new highways were built, including the [Nagano Expressway](/wiki/Nagano_Expressway "Nagano Expressway") and the [Jōshin-etsu Expressway](/wiki/J%C5%8Dshin-etsu_Expressway "Jōshin-etsu Expressway") and upgrades were made to existing roads.[[2]](#cite_note-transportation-3)

## Host city selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host city selection")]

Main article: [Bids for the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bids_for_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Bids for the 1998 Winter Olympics")

In 1932, Japan won the rights to host the [1940 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1940_Summer_Olympics "1940 Summer Olympics") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). At that time, organizers of the Summer Olympics had priority in choosing the venue for the Winter Olympics the same year.[[a 1]](#cite_note-4) Several Japanese cities, including Nagano, prepared a bid. Sapporo was chosen; however, the games never took place because of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[a 2]](#cite_note-5) In 1961, Nagano declared its intention to host the [1968 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics") but lost to Sapporo, the winning Japanese bid, who lost to [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), and Sapporo eventually won the right to host the [1972 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics").[[a 3]](#cite_note-6)

Japanese private sector organizations, in 1983, began publicly discussing a possible bid.[[a 4]](#cite_note-7) Two years later, in 1985, the Nagano Prefectural Assembly, decided to begin the process to bid, for its third time, for a Winter Olympics.[[a 5]](#cite_note-8) The bid committee was established in July 1986, they submitted their bid to the [Japanese Olympic Committee](/wiki/Japanese_Olympic_Committee "Japanese Olympic Committee") (JOC) in November of the same year. Other Japanese cities that were bidding were [Asahikawa](/wiki/Asahikawa "Asahikawa"), [Yamagata](/wiki/Yamagata,_Yamagata "Yamagata, Yamagata"), and [Morioka](/wiki/Morioka "Morioka").[[a 6]](#cite_note-9) 1 June 1988, the JOC selected Nagano in the first round of national voting, receiving 34 of 45 votes.[[a 7]](#cite_note-10) In 1989, the bid committee was reorganized, with the Japanese Prime Minister as head of the committee. The number of committee members was 511.[[a 8]](#cite_note-11)

On 12 February 1990, the bid delegation presented its candidature at the International Olympic Committee in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne") before Juan Antonio Samaranch.[[a 9]](#cite_note-12) Other candidate cities for the 1998 Olympics were [Aosta](/wiki/Aosta "Aosta"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"); [Jaca](/wiki/Jaca "Jaca"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"); [Östersund](/wiki/%C3%96stersund "Östersund"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"); [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"), and [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_SFSR "Russian SFSR"), [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (now [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")).[[a 10]](#cite_note-13) The host city selection was held in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), United Kingdom, on 15 June 1991, at the 97th [IOC session](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings").[[a 11]](#cite_note-14) After the first round of voting, Nagano led, with Aosta and Salt Lake City tied for last. Aosta was eliminated in a run-off against Salt Lake City. After the second round of voting, Nagano led with Salt Lake City in second, and Jaca was eliminated. Following round 3, Nagano continued to lead, with Salt Lake City in second, and Östersund was eliminated. Finally, Nagano prevailed over Salt Lake City by just 4 votes in the fifth round of voting, becoming the third Japanese city to host the games after Tokyo in [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") and Sapporo in 1972.[[a 12]](#cite_note-15) Nagano, at [36°N](/wiki/36th_parallel_north "36th parallel north"), is the southernmost city in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") to host the Winter Olympics ([1960 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1960_Winter_Olympics "1960 Winter Olympics") host [Squaw Valley, California](/wiki/Squaw_Valley,_Placer_County,_California "Squaw Valley, Placer County, California") is [39°N](/wiki/39th_parallel_north "39th parallel north")).[[a 13]](#cite_note-16) In June 1995, Salt Lake was chosen as the host of the following [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics").

Following a [2002 Winter Olympic bid scandal](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympic_bid_scandal "2002 Winter Olympic bid scandal") that occurred in the summer of 2000, [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), host of the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"), [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_(city) "Nagano (city)"), and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), host of the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics"), were suspected of similar improprieties in bidding practices. Although nothing illegal was ever done, gifts to IOC members were considered morally dubious.[[3]](#cite_note-17) The Nagano Olympic bid committee spent approximately $14 million to entertain the 62 [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") members and many of their companions. The precise figures are unknown since Nagano, after the IOC asked that the entertainment expenditures not be made public, destroyed the financial records, according to bid member [Junichi Yamaguchi](/wiki/Junichi_Yamaguchi "Junichi Yamaguchi").[[4]](#cite_note-18)[[5]](#cite_note-19)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Furuhashi_Hironoshin.JPG/250px-Furuhashi_Hironoshin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Furuhashi_Hironoshin.JPG)

[Furuhashi Hironoshin](/wiki/Furuhashi_Hironoshin "Furuhashi Hironoshin"), past president of the [JOC](/wiki/Japanese_Olympic_Committee "Japanese Olympic Committee")

Five months after the city was selected, the [Nagano Olympic Organizing Committee](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Organizing_Committee "Nagano Olympic Organizing Committee") (NAOC) was created. [Eishiro Saito](/wiki/Eishiro_Saito "Eishiro Saito"), Chairman of [Japan Business Federation](/wiki/Japan_Business_Federation "Japan Business Federation") (Keidanren) was selected as president of the committee. There were four Vice Presidents: [Goro Yoshimura](/wiki/Goro_Yoshimura "Goro Yoshimura"), the Governor of Nagano Prefecture; [Hironoshin Furuhashi](/wiki/Hironoshin_Furuhashi "Hironoshin Furuhashi"), president of the [Japanese Olympic Committee](/wiki/Japanese_Olympic_Committee "Japanese Olympic Committee"); [Yoshiaki Tsutsumi](/wiki/Yoshiaki_Tsutsumi "Yoshiaki Tsutsumi"), the president of the Ski Association of Japan; and [Tasuku Tsukada](/wiki/Tasuku_Tsukada "Tasuku Tsukada"), the Mayor of Nagano City.[[a 14]](#cite_note-21) In addition, the Vice Minister of the [Ministry of Home Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Home_Affairs_(Japan) "Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)"), Tadashi Tsuda, served as director-general.[[a 15]](#cite_note-22) Tsuda was replaced by [Makoto Kobayashi](/wiki/Makoto_Kobayashi_(Olympics) "Makoto Kobayashi (Olympics)") in 1993.[[a 16]](#cite_note-23)

The organizing committee recognized three goals for the games, which they referred to as "Games from the Heart": promote youth participation, coexistence with nature, create a festival with peace and friendship at its centre.[[a 17]](#cite_note-24) To realize the first goal, a camp bringing together 217 young people from 51 countries was created, along with the program of "One school, one country" in Nagano Prefecture.[[a 18]](#cite_note-25) This program organized cultural exchanges with other countries. In addition, more than 100,000 tickets were reserved for children.[[a 19]](#cite_note-26) For the second point, the organizers attempted to minimize the impact on their nature and the local ecosystem.[[a 20]](#cite_note-27) Regarding the third point, an international truce organized by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1997 was adopted during the games.[[a 21]](#cite_note-28)

> *The Nagano Olympics Games are a link to the 21st century, inspiring our search for wisdom for the new ear, respect for the beauty and bounty of nature, furtherance of peace and goodwill. Friends worldwide are welcome to share, in the spirit of competition and fair play, the joys and glory of the XVIII Olympic Winter Games.*[[a 22]](#cite_note-29)

In June 1998, four months after the Games, the NAOC presented a donation of US$1 million to the [Olympic Museum](/wiki/Olympic_Museum "Olympic Museum") in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"). This value come of the revenue of tickets sales and another actions from the committee.[[a 23]](#cite_note-30) In October of the same year, NAOC also donated the 3-D high vision theater and some structures that were used in Nagano Olympic Villages to the Olympic Museum.[[a 24]](#cite_note-31)

In February 1999, one year after the Games, the IOC awarded the Nagano the [Olympic Cup](/wiki/Olympic_Cup "Olympic Cup"), and presented the city a replica of the sculpture of stylized athletes raising the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag") by the [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") artist Nag Arnoldi.[[b 1]](#cite_note-32)

### Economic aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economic aspects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/M-Wave_northeast_corner_winter.jpg/250px-M-Wave_northeast_corner_winter.jpg)](/wiki/File:M-Wave_northeast_corner_winter.jpg)

[M-Wave](/wiki/M-Wave "M-Wave")

The costs of construction and of the land of the Olympic venues totaled ¥106.6 billion,[[a 25]](#cite_note-33) approximately 914 million [US dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar"). Of this, the [Japanese national government](/wiki/Government_of_Japan "Government of Japan") spent ¥51.1 billion, the [Nagano prefectural government](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture") spent ¥29.6 billion, and the cities and towns of Nagano, ¥23.4 billion; [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba "Hakuba"), ¥1 billion; and [Nozawa Onsen](/wiki/Nozawa_Onsen_Snow_Resort "Nozawa Onsen Snow Resort"), ¥1.1 billion; shared the remaining ¥25.5 billion.[[a 26]](#cite_note-34) The most expensive venue was impressive [M-Wave](/wiki/M-Wave "M-Wave"), the indoor rink which hosted the long-track speed skating events. It cost near ¥34.8 billion.[[a 27]](#cite_note-35) The two ice hockey venues, [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat") and [Aqua Wing Arena](/wiki/Aqua_Wing_Arena "Aqua Wing Arena") cost ¥19.1 and ¥9.1 billion respectively.[[a 28]](#cite_note-36) The [White Ring (arena)](/wiki/White_Ring_(arena) "White Ring (arena)"), which hosted figure skating and short-track speed skating cost ¥14.2 billion, the [Spiral](/wiki/Spiral_(bobsleigh,_luge,_and_skeleton) "Spiral (bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton)"), which hosted bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton, cost ¥10.1 billion.[[a 29]](#cite_note-37) Another ¥8.6 billion was spent on the [Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium](/wiki/Hakuba_Ski_Jumping_Stadium "Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium"), ¥7 billion for [Snow Harp](/wiki/Snow_Harp "Snow Harp") – the cross-country skiing venue, and ¥3 billion for the biathlon venue at [Nozawa Onsen Snow Resort](/wiki/Nozawa_Onsen_Snow_Resort "Nozawa Onsen Snow Resort").[[a 30]](#cite_note-38)

The organizing committee financed all costs, totaling ¥113.9 billion.[[a 31]](#cite_note-39) It spent ¥99.4 billion for operational expenses, ¥21.6 billion for public relations, ¥20.7 billion for installations, ¥18.4 billion for telecommunications, ¥15.9 billion for running the competitions, and ¥14.4 billion for administration.[[a 32]](#cite_note-40) Television rights were worth ¥35.4 billion, and marketing earned ¥31.3 billion.[[a 33]](#cite_note-41) Ticket sales were worth ¥10.5 billion.[[a 34]](#cite_note-42) The total cost of the Nagano Games is estimated to have been US$15.25 billion (in 2015), of which the largest factor in the cost of the games was the extension of the [shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") to Nagano. This compares, for example, with US$2.5 billion for the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), US$4.35 billion for the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), US$7.56 billion for the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics"), and US$51 billion for the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics")[[7]](#cite_note-43)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/E2_J6_Asama_Karuizawa_19980218.jpg/250px-E2_J6_Asama_Karuizawa_19980218.jpg)](/wiki/File:E2_J6_Asama_Karuizawa_19980218.jpg)

A [Nagano Shinkansen](/wiki/Nagano_Shinkansen "Nagano Shinkansen") [E2 Series](/wiki/E2_Series_Shinkansen "E2 Series Shinkansen") "J" set in February 1998

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Ueda-Roman_Bridge_1.jpg/250px-Ueda-Roman_Bridge_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ueda-Roman_Bridge_1.jpg)

[Jōshin-etsu Expressway](/wiki/J%C5%8Dshin-etsu_Expressway "Jōshin-etsu Expressway") in [Ueda, Nagano](/wiki/Ueda,_Nagano "Ueda, Nagano").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/%E6%B5%85%E5%B7%9D%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%A9%E3%82%A4%E3%83%B3.JPG/250px-%E6%B5%85%E5%B7%9D%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%A9%E3%82%A4%E3%83%B3.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E6%B5%85%E5%B7%9D%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%A9%E3%82%A4%E3%83%B3.JPG)

Asagawa Loop Line to [Iizuna Kogen Ski Area](/wiki/Iizuna_Kogen_Ski_Area "Iizuna Kogen Ski Area") built in preparations for the Games

[Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_(city) "Nagano (city)") is situated at the [Japanese Alps](/wiki/Japanese_Alps "Japanese Alps") area and receives large snowfalls every year. These combined to make transportation an important challenge for the organizing committee. In addition, the Olympic Village was a distance of 7 kilometers from the center of the city, and sporting events were spread over five surrounding cities. The complicating matters were that many of the venues had one single road in-out, which limited possibilities and led to traffic jams.[[a 35]](#cite_note-44)

To improve access to Nagano, the government decided to link the prefecture with the high-speed *[shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen")* train network. Called Nagano Shinkansen (now the [Hokuriku Shinkansen](/wiki/Hokuriku_Shinkansen "Hokuriku Shinkansen")), it was inaugurated five months before the start of the Games. This reduced by half the travel time between [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and Nagano, to 79 minutes for 221 kilometers.[[a 36]](#cite_note-45) The length of the track between [Takasaki Station](/wiki/Takasaki_Station "Takasaki Station") and [Nagano Station](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station") is 125.7 km, which includes 63.4 km of tunnels. The high speed train network carried 655,000 passengers during the Games.[[b 2]](#cite_note-46)

Two highways, the [Nagano Expressway](/wiki/Nagano_Expressway "Nagano Expressway") and the [Jōshin-etsu Expressway](/wiki/J%C5%8Dshin-etsu_Expressway "Jōshin-etsu Expressway"), were also built in the Nagano region.[[b 3]](#cite_note-47) In May 1993, the 75.8-kilometer section of the Nagano Expressway was completed, and in October 1997, the 111.4 kilometer section of the Jōshin-etsu Expressway was completed.[[b 4]](#cite_note-48) In addition, another 114.9 kilometers of roads within [Nagano Prefecture](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture") were improved.[[2]](#cite_note-transportation-3)

Transportation systems for the Games ran for 33 days, from the opening of the Athletes Village until 3 days after the Paralympics closing ceremony, when the Village was decommissioned from its functions. Approximately 64% of the athletes arrived between 1 and 6 February, and 74% left Nagano between 22 and 25 February.[[a 37]](#cite_note-49) Logistical operations were directed from the operational centre situated at the Organizing Committee headquarters. Two regional logistical minor hubs were created in [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba "Hakuba") and [Yamanouchi](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"), as well as a traffic center for buses and cars in [Karuizawa](/wiki/Karuizawa "Karuizawa").[[a 38]](#cite_note-50) Originally it was expected that both athletes and media delegations would arrive entirely via [Narita International Airport](/wiki/Narita_International_Airport "Narita International Airport"), which did not happen given the high demand for plane tickets. So some delegations had to arrive via [Kansai International Airport](/wiki/Kansai_International_Airport "Kansai International Airport") and [Chubu Centrair International Airport](/wiki/Chubu_Centrair_International_Airport "Chubu Centrair International Airport") in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya")[[a 39]](#cite_note-51) The members of the IOC and the delegations who arrived in Narita traveled to Nagano via [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen").[[a 40]](#cite_note-52)
To improve transportation for spectators, the number and hours of local trains were extended.[[a 41]](#cite_note-53) During the heaviest traffic days, more cars and buses were put in service and up to 68 parking areas, for 8,000 vehicles were at available for various Olympic delegations, and another 17 parking areas for 23,000 cars for spectators. Approximately 1,200 vehicles had navigation systems which transmitted their locations in a GPS system.[[a 42]](#cite_note-54)

As one of the principal aims of the Games was to respect nature, many vehicles were considered ecological or semi-ecological. In addition, there were more than 100 electric vehicles, hybrid mini-buses and other environmentally-friendly vehicles.[[a 43]](#cite_note-55)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Marketing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Stylized_Nagao_Olympics_manhole_cover_with_tactile_paving.jpg/250px-Stylized_Nagao_Olympics_manhole_cover_with_tactile_paving.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stylized_Nagao_Olympics_manhole_cover_with_tactile_paving.jpg)

Stylized manhole cover displaying the Nagano Olympics emblem, with [tactile paving](/wiki/Tactile_paving "Tactile paving")

The [emblem](/wiki/Emblem "Emblem") of the 1998 Winter Olympics consisted of a stylized snow flower with each petal representing an athlete participating in a winter sport. The figure could also represent a [snowflake](/wiki/Snowflake "Snowflake"), or a mountain flower, which refers to the importance of the natural environment to the city of Nagano. Similarly, [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") used [cherry blossoms](/wiki/Cherry_blossoms "Cherry blossoms") in its logo for its candidature for the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").

[Landor Associates](/wiki/Landor_Associates "Landor Associates") conceived the official mascots that were used by the communication team for the Games. They consisted of four owlets, [Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki](/wiki/Sukki,_Nokki,_Lekki_and_Tsukki "Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki"), also called *Snowlets*. The names were chosen from more than 47,000 suggestions. Four represents the number of years between each Olympic Games, and also represent the [four elements](/wiki/Four_elements "Four elements"), fire, air, earth, and water.

The official poster for the Games was designed by the [graphic designer](/wiki/Graphic_designer "Graphic designer") Masuteru Aoba presented a [thrush](/wiki/Thrush_(bird) "Thrush (bird)") perched on ski poles with light in the background shining on snow-capped mountain peaks. Here, as with the emblem and the mascots, the importance of the natural environment in these Olympic Games and a desire to create harmony between athletes and the natural surroundings are shown. In addition to the official poster, a separate poster was created for the opening ceremony.[[a 44]](#cite_note-56) Marketing for the games cost the organizing committee 5.9 billion yen.[[a 45]](#cite_note-57)

These Olympic Games were sponsored by 11 worldwide partners, 8 gold partners, and 18 official supports and suppliers. Marketing revenues for sponsoring or for the rights to use the emblems and mascots of the Games totaled 31.3 billion yen.[[a 46]](#cite_note-58)

#### Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mascots")]

Main article: [Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki](/wiki/Sukki,_Nokki,_Lekki_and_Tsukki "Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki")

Sukki, Nokki, Lekki and Tsukki, also known as the Snowlets, are the 1998 Winter Olympic mascots and are four snowy owls. They represent respectively fire (Sukki), air (Nokki), earth (Lekki) and water (Tsukki) and together they represent the four major islands of Japan.

#### Sponsors of the 1998 Winter Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sponsors of the 1998 Winter Olympics")]

The development of Rights Packages were based on International Olympic Committee policy of offering exclusive rights to a limited number of companies, with one company allowed to purchase the rights for any single product or service category, and these were based on previous Games, with adaptations for the local market.[[a 47]](#cite_note-59) Sponsors were permitted to use the emblem and mascots as long as consent was obtained from the International Olympic Committee, [Japanese Olympic Committee](/wiki/Japanese_Olympic_Committee "Japanese Olympic Committee"), and the NAOC.[[a 48]](#cite_note-60) Hospitality packages for sponsors included priority for accommodations, tickets, and transportation services.[[a 49]](#cite_note-61) The Sponsor Hospitality Village, next to the [Nagano Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Stadium "Nagano Olympic Stadium"), welcomed 32,000 guests.[[a 50]](#cite_note-62)

To promote awareness of the sponsors, advertising was done in various media from 1995, and on banners and buses immediately before the games.[[a 51]](#cite_note-63) [Dick Pound](/wiki/Dick_Pound "Dick Pound") noted, during the Games, the excellence of the marketing program, citing the "*perfect example of how the private and public sectors can work together*".[[a 52]](#cite_note-64)

### Ticket sales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ticket sales")]

From 7 February 1997, the organizing committee put up for sale 1,286,000 tickets for the various competitions and ceremonies. The number of tickets sold was 1,149,615, which represented 89.4% of available tickets. Including people connected to the Games, the total number of spectators was 1,275,529. This number was slightly higher than in 1994 as a result of the greater availability of tickets. This reflected the total capacity of the arenas used, which were mostly larger than those used four years earlier but slightly lower than the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"). Tickets sales were a success in the domestic market, as the Japanese people finished the process of purchase with a reservation list of 6 million. For the most popular events, a lottery system was used.[[a 54]](#cite_note-66) In total, ticket sales raised 10.5 billion yen for the organizing committee.[[a 55]](#cite_note-67)

The [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics") matches represented 295,802 tickets sold, 26% of the total. Tickets sold for [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") totaled 166,092; for [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ski jumping at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), 96,000, and [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), 93,000. For multiple sports, ski jumping, [Nordic combined](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Nordic combined at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [freestyle skiing](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), all three skating disciplines, [bobsleigh](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), as well as the ceremonies, all tickets were sold. By contrast, due the local demands and low interest only 56.6% of the 146,000 available tickets for [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") were sold.[[a 56]](#cite_note-68)

### Cost and cost overrun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cost and cost overrun")]

*The Oxford Olympics Study* established the outturn cost of the Nagano 1998 Winter Olympics at US$2.2 billion in 2015-dollars and cost overrun at 56% in real terms.[[8]](#cite_note-69) This includes sports-related costs only, that is, (i) *operational costs* incurred by the organizing committee for the purpose of staging the Games, e.g., expenditures for technology, transportation, workforce, administration, security, catering, ceremonies, and medical services, and (ii) *direct capital costs* incurred by the host city and country or private investors to build, e.g., the competition venues, the Olympic village, international broadcast center, and media and press center, which are required to host the Games. Indirect capital costs are *not* included, such as for road, rail, or airport infrastructure, or for hotel upgrades or other business investment incurred in preparation for the Games but not directly related to staging the Games. The cost and cost overrun for Nagano 1998 compares with costs of US$2.5 billion and a cost overrun of 13% for Vancouver 2010, and costs of US$51[[9]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-70) billion and a cost overrun of 289% for Sochi 2014, the latter being the most costly Olympics to date. Average cost for Winter Games since 1960 is US$3.1 billion, average cost overrun is 142%.

### Sport sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sport sites")]

During the 1998 Winter Olympics, a total of fourteen sports venues, all within [Nagano Prefecture](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture"), were used. Construction of these venues lasted 7 years between 1990 and 1997, with construction and land costs totaling ¥106.6 billion.[[a 57]](#cite_note-71) The most expensive venue constructed for the games was the [M-Wave](/wiki/M-Wave "M-Wave") who hosted the speed skating events.This venue is in a distance from 5 kilometers from [Nagano Station](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station"). Between March 1996 and November 1997, these sites were tested with 16 different World Championship events, World Cups, and another international competitions to allow the organizers to prepare for the running of the Games.[[a 58]](#cite_note-72)

Five new venues were used, localized all inside [city of Nagano urban area](/wiki/Nagano_(city) "Nagano (city)"). The project to build the Olympic Stadium and the 4 indoor arenas was planned even before the plans for the Olympic bid. However, these plans were accelerated soon after the submission of the proposal to the Japanese Olympic Committee, as the city had no infrastructure available for any type of event available. The [Nagano Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Stadium "Nagano Olympic Stadium") is the main sporting venue of the south of city, is on nearby [Shinonoi Station](/wiki/Shinonoi_Station "Shinonoi Station"), and approximately 9 kilometers from [Nagano Station](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station"). Outside from their structure, the stadium, who resembles a [cherry blossom](/wiki/Cherry_blossom "Cherry blossom"), one of main symbols of Japan.During the Games, the arena was exclusively used for the opening and closing ceremonies and, when added to the temporary infrastructure, its capacity reached 50.000.[[b 5]](#cite_note-73)

The [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat") Arena was the [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics") main venue.is approximately located 2 km from Nagano Station and have a capacity of 10,104 spectators.[[b 6]](#cite_note-74) Another arena, the
[Aqua Wing Arena](/wiki/Aqua_Wing_Arena "Aqua Wing Arena"), the second ice hockey venue. Shaped like a wing, it had a capacity of 6000 during the Olympics. After the Games, it was modified into a public indoor swimming with a 25m pool.[[b 7]](#cite_note-75) Aqua Wing is approximately 5 kilometers from Nagano Station. Its closest stations are [Kita-Nagano Station](/wiki/Kita-Nagano_Station "Kita-Nagano Station") and [Asahi Station](/wiki/Asahi_Station_(Nagano) "Asahi Station (Nagano)"). M-Wave, the [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics") venue, is the first indoor track speed skating venue in Japan. It was built to accommodate 10,000 spectators.[[b 8]](#cite_note-76) The venue, which gets its name from its M-shape, representing the surrounding mountains and harmonize with the skyline, is approximately 5 kilometers from Nagano Station. Finally, [White Ring](/wiki/White_Ring_(arena) "White Ring (arena)"), with a maximum capacity of 7.351 spectators, was built for [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [short track speed skating](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Short track speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics").[[b 9]](#cite_note-77) White Ring, which is used as a public gymnasium, is approximately 6 kilometers from Nagano Station.

[Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba,_Nagano "Hakuba, Nagano") village is situated 50 kilometers west of the city of Nagano. Hakuba hosted three Olympics venues [Alpine skiing's](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") downhill, super-G and combined were situated at [Happo'one Resort](/wiki/Happo%27one_Resort "Happo'one Resort"). Three courses between altitudes of 840 meters and 1,765 meters were used, one for the men's, women's and combined for both men's and women's. The site had a capacity of 20,000 spectators.[[b 10]](#cite_note-78) [Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium](/wiki/Hakuba_Ski_Jumping_Stadium "Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium") was the first ski jump built in Japan with parallel 90 and 120 K-point hills. The ski jumping stadium could accommodate 45,000 spectators.[[b 11]](#cite_note-79) [Snow Harp](/wiki/Snow_Harp "Snow Harp") Kamishiro was built for [cross country skiing and Nordic combined](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics"). It includes three tracks of 4.8, 4.8, and 7.8 kilometers.The stadium area is near 1.2 km2. In total, Snow Harp has 19 kilometers of tracks. Up to 20,000 spectators could be accommodated.[[b 12]](#cite_note-80)

[Nozawa Onsen Ski Resort](/wiki/Nozawa_Onsen_Ski_Resort "Nozawa Onsen Ski Resort"), in the town of [Nozawaonsen](/wiki/Nozawaonsen,_Nagano "Nozawaonsen, Nagano"), was site of [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Biathlon at the 1998 Winter Olympics"). Nozawa is approximately 50 kilometers north of Nagano. At Nozawa Onsen, the stadium was built around six existing tracks. Two tracks, of 4 kilometers and 7 kilometers, were used for the Games. The stadium could accommodate 20,000 spectators.[[b 13]](#cite_note-81)

Two sites in the town of [Yamanouchi](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"), approximately 30 kilometers northeast of Nagano, were used. Giant slalom was held at [Mount Yakebitai](/wiki/Mount_Yakebitai "Mount Yakebitai") at [Shiga Kogen Resort](/wiki/Shiga_Highlands "Shiga Highlands"), at an altitude between 1,530 and 1,969 meters. The site could accommodate 20,000 spectators. Also in Shiga Kogen, at [Mount Higashidate](/wiki/Mount_Higashidate "Mount Higashidate"), giant slalom events in Alpine skiing and snowboarding were held.[[b 14]](#cite_note-82) [Kanbayashi Snowboard Park](/wiki/Kanbayashi_Snowboard_Park "Kanbayashi Snowboard Park") was the site of the [half pipe event in snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics") . The track is 120 meters long and 15 meters wide, with walls of 3.5 meters. 10,000 spectators could be accommodated at Kanbayashi.[[b 15]](#cite_note-83)

The town of [Iizuna](/wiki/Iizuna,_Nagano "Iizuna, Nagano"), approximately 12 kilometers northwest of Nagano, was the site of [freestyle skiing](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton](/wiki/Spiral_(bobsleigh,_luge,_and_skeleton) "Spiral (bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton)") at [Iizuna Kogen Ski Area](/wiki/Iizuna_Kogen_Ski_Area "Iizuna Kogen Ski Area"). 8,000 spectators could watch the free style skiing on a course that 250 meters long and 12,000 could the jumps.[[b 16]](#cite_note-84) The Spiral, which held the sledding events, was the first artificially refrigerated track in Asia. It is 1700 meters long, with a difference in height of 114 meters and 15 turns. At the Spiral, approximately 40,000 [saplings](/wiki/Sapling "Sapling"), mainly [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech") and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), were planted two per square meter, as part of the environmental stewardship committed during the Winter Games. The site could accommodate 10,000 spectators.[[b 17]](#cite_note-85)

Finally, the town of [Karuizawa](/wiki/Karuizawa "Karuizawa"), approximately 80 kilometers southeast of Nagano, hosted the [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics") events at [Kazakoshi Park Arena](/wiki/Kazakoshi_Park_Arena "Kazakoshi Park Arena"). The venue was built as a multi-purpose venue. Its ice surface is 60 meters by 30. Its maximum capacity is 1,924 spectators.[[b 18]](#cite_note-86) The same venue also hosted the [equestrian events](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 1964 Summer Olympics") at the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics"), thus becoming the first venue to host both the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") and [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics").

### Accommodation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Accommodation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Asahi_Danchi_with_M-Wave.jpg/250px-Asahi_Danchi_with_M-Wave.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asahi_Danchi_with_M-Wave.jpg)

Media Village at Asahi, with the [M-Wave](/wiki/M-Wave "M-Wave") in the background

To accommodate the athletes and officials during the Games, the main [Olympic Village](/wiki/Olympic_Village "Olympic Village") was constructed in Imai district, this complex approximately 7 kilometers south of Nagano Station. Along with the construction of the village, [Imai Station](/wiki/Imai_Station "Imai Station") was opened in 1997. The responsibility for the construction of these buildings lay with the Nagano City, as a future public and low coast residential housing and was loaned to the organizing committee during the Games.[[b 19]](#cite_note-87) The main Village occupies an area that is 19 hectares, composed of 23 buildings with a total of 1,032 apartments.[[b 20]](#cite_note-88) Temporary infrastrure were also available during the Games. The Village was open for the Olympics from 24 January to 25 February 1998 and 1 to 19 March for the Paralympics, and accommodated nearby 4,000 people during the two events.[[b 21]](#cite_note-89) Several prominent people were recognized as faces of the Olympic Village, including the *Honorary Mayor* [Yasuko Konoe](/wiki/Yasuko_Konoe "Yasuko Konoe"), *Mayor* [Shozo Sasahara](/wiki/Shozo_Sasahara "Shozo Sasahara"), and *Deputy Mayors* [Takanori Kono](/wiki/Takanori_Kono "Takanori Kono"), [Hiroko Chiba](/w/index.php?title=Hiroko_Chiba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hiroko Chiba (page does not exist)"), and [Shunichi Bobby Hirai](/w/index.php?title=Shunichi_Bobby_Hirai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shunichi Bobby Hirai (page does not exist)").[[b 22]](#cite_note-90)

As the curling venue was in Karuizawa, 90 kilometers away, a satellite village was built in Karuizawa, 7 kilometers from the arena.[[b 23]](#cite_note-91) It was open from 4 to 16 February 1998. In addition, a section of the Shiga Kogen [Resort](/wiki/Prince_Hotels "Prince Hotels"), 58 kilometers from the Olympic Village, was reserved for 180 snowboarders and officials.[[b 24]](#cite_note-92)

In addition to athletes and officials, members of the Olympic and Paralympic family and other personnel were housed in 900 hotels and another structures in Nagano and surrounding regions, which represented 234,207 nights between 24 January to 25 February 1998. The Olympic and Paralympic families stayed in the Kokusai 21 Hotel in downtown Nagano. In total, the Olympic and Paralympic families included 18,350 people. Finally, two media villages were built in the districts of [Yanagimachi](/wiki/Yanagimachi "Yanagimachi"), near Nagano Station, and Asahi, across the street from the M-Wave.[[a 59]](#cite_note-93)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Games")]

### The Olympic torch relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Olympic torch relay")]

Main article: [1998 Winter Olympics torch relay](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics_torch_relay "1998 Winter Olympics torch relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Nagano_torch.JPG/250px-Nagano_torch.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nagano_torch.JPG)

The Nagano Olympic torch, displayed at the Olympic Museum in Nagano

The [Olympic torch](/wiki/Olympic_torch "Olympic torch") was lit by sunlight during the ceremony held [Temple of Hera](/wiki/Temple_of_Hera_(Olympia) "Temple of Hera (Olympia)") at [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") on 19 December 1997. [Vassilis Dimitriadis](/wiki/Vassilis_Dimitriadis "Vassilis Dimitriadis"), a Greek alpine skier started the Greek leg towards [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") where the handover ceremony was held at the [Panathenaic Stadium](/wiki/Panathenaic_Stadium "Panathenaic Stadium"). The flame burned from 27 to 30 December in the [Ginza](/wiki/Ginza "Ginza") yard, and from 31 December to 4 January in the [Yoyogi Park](/wiki/Yoyogi_Park "Yoyogi Park"). On the morning on that day the flame was split into three and taken by plane to three destinations: the [Kantō](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D "Kantō") route, starting from [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido"); the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") route, starting from [Kagoshima](/wiki/Kagoshima "Kagoshima"); and the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") route starting in [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture"). On 23 January evening, the three flames arrived on [Nagano Prefecture](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture") after 120 stops. Finally, these three routes were unified in the city of Nagano in on the night of 6 February. The following day, after traveling through each district of the city, the relays simultaneously arrived at the central square where three former athletes passed the flames to three members of the organizing committee who lit a celebration cauldron gathering the flame into one. After that, the unified flame was used to light another torch held by [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch"). In the early hours of the 7 February morning, the final leg started and the flame travelled another 10 kilometers to the Olympic Stadium and the [figure skater](/wiki/Figure_skater "Figure skater") [Midori Ito](/wiki/Midori_Ito "Midori Ito") lit the cauldron at [Nagano Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Stadium "Nagano Olympic Stadium") during the opening ceremonies.

The Olympic Flame Relay in Japan was sponsored by [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), lasted 33 days and travelled 1,162 kilometers. A group of 5.5 million people took part in relay activities. Over the distance of the relay, the flame was always followed by a group of six people: the torchbearer who carried the flame, some who accompanied the carrier, and four people in supporting roles, for a total of 6,901 people. In addition, each leg was followed by two groups of 11 vehicles and more than 20 people.

The torch was shaped like a traditional Japanese *taimatsu* torch. It was made of aluminum, was 55 centimeters long, and weighed 1.3 kilograms when it was assembled. The exterior of the torch was coloured silver, to represent winter. Runners wore blue and white uniforms mixing blue as the main colour of the look of the games and the white as snow. The runners' uniforms included logos for the Nagano Olympics and the Olympic Games, a logo of the relay, and of Coca-Cola.

### Participating National Olympic Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Participating National Olympic Committees")]

72 nations participated in the 1998 Winter Olympic Games for a total of 2,176 athletes, of which 787 were female and 1,389 were male. With the addition of five countries and another 439 athletes since the [1994 Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympic_Games "1994 Winter Olympic Games") at [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), these were the largest Winter Olympics ever at the time. The nations of [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Azerbaijan at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Kenya at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Macedonia at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Uruguay at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Venezuela at the 1998 Winter Olympics") participated in their first Winter Olympic Games. [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") returned to the Winter games after a 22-year absence, and [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "North Korea at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [India](/wiki/India_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "India at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ireland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") returned after 6 years. [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Yugoslavia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") appeared again as the [Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"). Five countries, [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"), [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), and [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), which were at the 1994 Games, did not participate in 1998.

The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") had the largest athlete delegation with 186, followed by host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") with 156, [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") with 144, and [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") with 125. Despite the large number of participating delegations, 40 of the 72 delegations had less than 10 athletes, with 12 nations having one sole athlete. 15 nations had between 11 and 50 athletes, 11 nations had between 51 and 100 athletes, and six nations had more than 101 athletes. Nations that participated in the ice hockey tournaments generally had the largest athlete delegations. With the exception of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), all 12 national delegations with 60 or more athletes participated in either or both of the female or male ice hockey tournaments.

### Calendar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Calendar")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg/250px-Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)

The men's [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics") gold medal game: Russia vs Czech Republic.

The 1998 Winter Olympics were held in a period of 15 days, from 7–22 February. The number of events increased from 61 at the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics") to 68 in 1998. Two sports, [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics") were added to the program, as was [women's ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament"). This increased the number of sports to seven, and the number of disciplines to 14. The sporting program started and ended with men's ice hockey.The first matches started at 4:00 pm on 7 February featuring [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Kazakhstan men's national ice hockey team") defeating [Italy](/wiki/Italy_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Italy men's national ice hockey team") 5–3, and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovakia men's national ice hockey team") tying [Austria](/wiki/Austria_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Austria men's national ice hockey team") 2–2. The final match was played on Sunday 22 February from 1:45 pm, and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") defeated [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") 1–0.The first final was held at the morning of day 8 in the [Cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") .Due to adverse weather conditions, the schedule for multiple events were delayed or changed, including six [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") events, snowboarding, and [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Biathlon at the 1998 Winter Olympics").

#### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main article: [1998 Winter Olympics opening ceremony](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremony "1998 Winter Olympics opening ceremony")

The opening ceremony took place at the [Nagano Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Stadium "Nagano Olympic Stadium"), Nagano, Japan, on the late morning of 7 February 1998.[[11]](#cite_note-95) Japanese figure skater, [Midori Ito](/wiki/Midori_Ito "Midori Ito"), the first female skater to land seven triple jumps in a free skating competition and also the first Asian world champion in 1989, and the silver medalist at the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics"), lit the cauldron during the ceremony.

[Seiji Ozawa](/wiki/Seiji_Ozawa "Seiji Ozawa"), a Japanese [conductor](/wiki/Conducting "Conducting"), directed an orchestra from five continents ([Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") - and the crowd in the Nagano Olympic Stadium),[[12]](#cite_note-96) to perform the fourth movement of [Beethoven's Symphony No. 9](/wiki/Symphony_No._9_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)") (Ode to Joy).[[13]](#cite_note-:0-97)[[14]](#cite_note-:1-98) This was the first time a simultaneous international audio-visual performance had been achieved and was also the last time that the Olympics Opening ceremonies were held on a morning timeslot in the host city.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-97)[[14]](#cite_note-:1-98)[[15]](#cite_note-99)

In all, 2,302 athletes from 72 National Olympic Committees participated in the Games,[[16]](#cite_note-100) including 814 female athletes and 1488 male athletes. Both the number of participating delegations and the number of athletes participating in the competition were, at the time, the most ever hosted at the Winter Olympics.

#### Medal ceremonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Medal ceremonies")]

The version used at the time of the Olympic Charter, approved on September 5, 1997.

The Olympic Charter in force at that time determined that (skating, ice hockey, and curling) were had to be at the event venues immediately after the finalsThe version used at the time of the Olympic Charter, approved on September 5, 1997. Determined that only award ceremonies for indoor events and those scheduled for the last day of competitions could be held immediately after the end of the respective events, while those relating to outdoor events would be held in two parts, the first carried out with the delivery of bouquets or gifts and the second on the same night with the delivery of medals in a public square.In Nagano, this plaza was localizated at the Nagano City Central Square, approximately midway between the city's main train stations:[Nagano Station](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station") and [Zenkō-ji](/wiki/Zenk%C5%8D-ji "Zenkō-ji") Temple.[[b 25]](#cite_note-101)[[b 26]](#cite_note-102) For the medal ceremonies, a total 167,200 tickets were sold. National and international artists presented every evening at the place before the medal presentations.[[b 27]](#cite_note-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG/250px-1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG)](/wiki/File:1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG)

The silver, gold and bronze medals of Nagano 1998

The gold, silver, and bronze medals each measured 80 mm in diameter and 9.7 mm in thickness.[[b 28]](#cite_note-104) The gold medals weighed 256 [g](/wiki/Gram "Gram"), the silver 250 g, and the bronze 230 g.[[b 29]](#cite_note-105) The medals were made using a traditional Japanese lacquerware technique known as 漆器 (*shikki*), in which a brass core is imprinted with the design by layering gold powder onto the wet lacquer using a method called [maki-e](/wiki/Maki-e "Maki-e").[[b 30]](#cite_note-106) On the front of the medals are borders of olive leaves, and in the center, a *maki-e* morning sun rises over a [cloisonné](/wiki/Cloisonn%C3%A9 "Cloisonné") emblem of the Nagano Olympics.[[b 31]](#cite_note-107) On the reverse side, the snowflower emblem of the Games sits above a *maki-e* image of the mountains surrounding Nagano glowing in the morning sunrise.[[b 32]](#cite_note-108) The initial lacquering was handcrafted by artisans from the town [Kiso, Nagano](/wiki/Kiso,_Nagano_(town) "Kiso, Nagano (town)"), and the metals components were added at the [Mint Bureau](/wiki/Japan_Mint "Japan Mint") of the [Japanese Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Japan) "Ministry of Finance (Japan)").[[b 33]](#cite_note-109)

In addition to the medals awarded to the athletes in each event, more than 19,000 commemorative medals were given to all athletes, officials, International Olympic Committee members, media personnel, volunteers and others. These medals, made by the Mint Bureau in cooperation with NAOC, were made from an [alloy](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") of 90% [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and 10% [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc").[[b 34]](#cite_note-110) As the Olympic Charter also determines, [diplomas](/wiki/Diplomas "Diplomas"), written in [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), were given to the top eight finishers in each event, and every participant also received a commemorative diploma.[[b 35]](#cite_note-111)

#### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [1998 Winter Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics_closing_ceremony "1998 Winter Olympics closing ceremony")

The closing ceremonies, like those of the opening, took place in the [Nagano Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Stadium "Nagano Olympic Stadium"), with 50,000 tickets sold. [Akihito](/wiki/Akihito "Akihito"), the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") at the time, and his wife [Empress Michiko](/wiki/Empress_Michiko "Empress Michiko") were also present. After the athletes entered, hundreds of drums were beat and a traditional hose and lion dance was presented. During the [Antwerp Ceremony](/w/index.php?title=Antwerp_Ceremony&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antwerp Ceremony (page does not exist)"), [Tasuku Tsukada](/wiki/Tasuku_Tsukada "Tasuku Tsukada"), then mayor of [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_(city) "Nagano (city)") presented the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag") to [Deedee Corradini](/wiki/Deedee_Corradini "Deedee Corradini"), then mayor of [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), the host of the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics").After the handover of the flag, the then IOC president [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch") declared the games closed. This was followed by a performance from the Japanese singer [Anri](/wiki/Anri "Anri") when the Olympic Cauldron was extinguished. The ceremony ended with the words "*Sayonara, Arigato*" ("Goodbye, Thank you"), a major [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks") performance accompanied by a party with the song: "Ile Aiye" performed by Japanese pop group Agharta.[[17]](#cite_note-112)

In all, 24 of the 72 participating National Olympic Committees at these Games won at least one medal, as shown in the table below. A total of 15 countries won at least one gold medal and 18 nations won two or more medals. In total, 205 medals were distributed. [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") finished on top of the table with 29 medals, including 12 gold, nine silver, and eight bronze. Germany, which finished in third place in the medal standings at the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), won most of its medals in Alpine skiing, speed skating, and luge, in which it won all three gold medals. German female athletes won 22 of the country's 29 medals. [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") finished again in second, with 25 medals, including nine won in cross-country skiing and five in biathlon. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), which finished atop the gold medals in 1994, finished in third in 1998, with 9 gold medals, including the five gold at the stake on the women's cross-country skiing.Result of the addition of new sports and events, [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") moved from a discret seventh in 1994 to fourth in 1998 with 6 gold medals, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") remained again in fifth place.The [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Netherlands at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), which had a discreet campaign in 1994, ended the 1998 Games with the best performance in its history, winning half of the 10 events contested in speed skating and totaling more than a third of the medals up for grabs. Host [The host country, Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") had a historic campaign. The country, which until then had only won 3 gold medals in the history of the Winter Games, finished the Games with 5.The japanese team also won 1 silver and 4 bronze medals, in a total of 10. [Australia](/wiki/Australia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Australia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") became the second country from the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") to win a medal, a bronze in alpine skiing. Also, [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Denmark at the 1998 Winter Olympics") each won your first ever medal, the silver at women's curling. In addition, [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Bulgaria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1998 Winter Olympics") each won their first gold medals at a Winter Olympics in women's biathlon and men's ice hockey respectively. Finally, [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Kazakhstan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won its first medal from a female athlete.
