## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Sports")]

### Olympic Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Olympic Program")]

The 1998 Winter Olympics featured a record 68 medal events over 14 disciplines in seven sports. In relation to the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), seven new events were added to the Winter Olympic program. Officially absent since the [1924 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"), curling returned to the Olympic program (the sport was demonstrated in 1932,1988 and 1992)
with 2 events (one tournament for men and one for women). For the first time, an ice hockey tournament for women was included. Another change was the addition of another discipline in alpine skiing: snowboarding with 4 events.

### Biathlon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Biathlon")]

Main article: [Biathlon at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Biathlon at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Uschi_disl_2006.jpg/250px-Uschi_disl_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uschi_disl_2006.jpg)

[Uschi Disl](/wiki/Uschi_Disl "Uschi Disl") of Germany, won one gold, one silver, and one bronze in the biathlon.

The [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon") competitions took place at [Nozawa Onsen Snow Resort](/wiki/Nozawa_Onsen_Snow_Resort "Nozawa Onsen Snow Resort"), north of Nagano City. The six events were the sprint, individual, and relay, for both men and women. In all, 183 athletes took part, including 96 men and 87 women from 32 countries. [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") each won five medals, with [Uschi Disl](/wiki/Uschi_Disl "Uschi Disl") from the latter country winning one gold, one silver, and one bronze.

The first event was the women's 15 km individual race that took place in falling snow on 9 February. The surprise gold medalist was [Ekaterina Dafovska](/wiki/Ekaterina_Dafovska "Ekaterina Dafovska") from [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Bulgaria at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), who had been ranked 51st at the previous [Biathlon World Cup](/wiki/Biathlon_World_Cup "Biathlon World Cup"). Her gold medal was the first-ever Bulgarian gold medal at a Winter Olympics. Her time was 54:52.0, with only one target missed. [Olena Petrova](/wiki/Olena_Petrova "Olena Petrova") from [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ukraine at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won the silver, 17.8 seconds behind, and Uschi Disl won the bronze, 25.9 seconds behind Dafovska.

The first men's event, the 20 km individual race, took place on 11 February. The [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Halvard Hanevold](/wiki/Halvard_Hanevold "Halvard Hanevold") missed his second-last target, but finished first in a time of 56:16.4. The [Italian](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Pieralberto Carrara](/wiki/Pieralberto_Carrara "Pieralberto Carrara"), who missed no targets, target, won the silver, 5.05 seconds behind. The [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Alexei Aidarov](/wiki/Alexei_Aidarov "Alexei Aidarov") was 30.1 seconds behind the Norwegian, and won the bronze.

### Bobsleigh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Bobsleigh")]

Main article: [Bobsleigh at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [bobsleigh](/wiki/Bobsleigh "Bobsleigh") competitions took place at [the Spiral](/wiki/Spiral_(bobsleigh,_luge,_and_skeleton) "Spiral (bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton)"), in [Iizuna](/wiki/Iizuna,_Nagano "Iizuna, Nagano"), just north of Nagano City. The Spiral course measured 1700 m in length, with fifteen curves, descended 113 m from start to finish, and included two short uphill sections. The two events were the two-man and four-man, for men only. Female competitors would begin competing in the [two woman](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Two-woman "Bobsleigh at the 2002 Winter Olympics – Two-woman") events at the subsequent Winter Olympics, the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") in Salt Lake City.

In all, 156 athletes took part from 28 countries. The bobsleigh events resulted in two ties, for the two-man gold and for the four-man bronze. This was the first time in Olympic bobsleigh history that there were ties for the medal positions. [Christoph Langen](/wiki/Christoph_Langen "Christoph Langen") and [Markus Zimmermann](/wiki/Markus_Zimmermann "Markus Zimmermann") won bronze in the two-man competition and were part of the winning four-man team. In all, [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") win one gold and one bronze; [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") also won one gold each when the two-man team. Six team in all won medals. The first time since the [1968 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics") did more than four countries win bobsleigh medals. In addition, Germany and Switzerland were the only two countries to place two sleds in the top ten of either event.[[18]](#cite_note-bobsleigh_sr-113)

The 1996 and 1997 [Bobsleigh World Champions](/wiki/IBSF_World_Championships_(bobsleigh_and_skeleton) "IBSF World Championships (bobsleigh and skeleton)") were teams from Germany and Italy respectively. However, [Günther Huber](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Huber "Günther Huber") and [Antonio Tartaglia](/wiki/Antonio_Tartaglia "Antonio Tartaglia") from Italy tied with the two-man team from Canada, [Pierre Lueders](/wiki/Pierre_Lueders "Pierre Lueders") and [Dave MacEachern](/wiki/Dave_MacEachern "Dave MacEachern") for the gold medal, each with combined times of 3:37.24. No silver medal was awarded. The German team of Christoph Langen and Markus Zimmermann were 0.65 seconds behind, and were awarded the bronze.

In the four-man event, bad weather restricted the competition to three runs only. The German team of Christoph Langen, Markus Zimmermann, [Marco Jakobs](/wiki/Marco_Jakobs "Marco Jakobs") and [Olaf Hampel](/wiki/Olaf_Hampel "Olaf Hampel") completed the three runs in 2:39.41 for the gold medal. The Swiss team of [Marcel Rohner](/wiki/Marcel_Rohner_(bobsleigh) "Marcel Rohner (bobsleigh)"), [Markus Nüssli](/wiki/Markus_N%C3%BCssli "Markus Nüssli"), [Markus Wasser](/wiki/Markus_Wasser "Markus Wasser"), and [Beat Seitz](/wiki/Beat_Seitz "Beat Seitz") finished second with a time of 2:40.01. Two teams, were awarded bronze medals after completing the three runs in 2:40.06. These were the team from Great Britain, made up of [Sean Olsson](/wiki/Sean_Olsson "Sean Olsson"), [Dean Ward](/wiki/Dean_Ward_(bobsleigh) "Dean Ward (bobsleigh)"), [Courtney Rumbolt](/wiki/Courtney_Rumbolt "Courtney Rumbolt"), and [Paul Attwood](/wiki/Paul_Attwood "Paul Attwood"); and the team from France, composed of [Bruno Mingeon](/wiki/Bruno_Mingeon "Bruno Mingeon"), [Emmanuel Hostache](/wiki/Emmanuel_Hostache "Emmanuel Hostache"), [Éric Le Chanony](/wiki/%C3%89ric_Le_Chanony "Éric Le Chanony"), and [Max Robert](/wiki/Max_Robert "Max Robert").

### Curling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Curling")]

Main article: [Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[Curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling") was included in the program for the Nagano Olympics in 1993 following discussions that had begun in 1992. At the time, it was considered that curling was making its official Olympic debut following its appearance as a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the [1932](/wiki/1932_Winter_Olympics "1932 Winter Olympics"), [1988](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics"), and [1992](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics"). At the Games in Nagano, both the men's and the women's curling tournament took place at [Kazakoshi Park Arena](/wiki/Kazakoshi_Park_Arena "Kazakoshi Park Arena") in [Karuizawa, Nagano](/wiki/Karuizawa,_Nagano "Karuizawa, Nagano"), 30 minutes by [bullet train (*shinkansen*)](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") south of Nagano City. Eight teams played a total of seven games in the round robin in both tournaments, with the four best teams going to the semifinals. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won gold in the women's competition and silver in the men's; [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won the gold in the men's tournament.

In the men's tournament, the [Mike Harris](/wiki/Mike_Harris_(curler) "Mike Harris (curler)") team from Canada easily completed the [round-robin tournament](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") winning six of its seven games, only losing to the [Eigil Ramsfjell](/wiki/Eigil_Ramsfjell "Eigil Ramsfjell") team from Norway. In the semi-finals, the Canadian team defeated [Tim Somerville's team](/wiki/Tim_Somerville "Tim Somerville") from the [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America") by a score of 7–1; and in the other semi-final, the team from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") led by [Patrick Hürlimann](/wiki/Patrick_H%C3%BCrlimann "Patrick Hürlimann") defeated Norway 8–7. In the gold medal game, Switzerland shocked Canada by winning 9–3. In the bronze medal game, Eigil Ramsfjell's team from Norway defeated Tim Somerville's USA team by a score of 9–4.

In the women's tournament, the [Sandra Schmirler](/wiki/Sandra_Schmirler "Sandra Schmirler") team from Canada and the [Elisabet Gustafson](/wiki/Elisabet_Gustafson "Elisabet Gustafson") team from Sweden easily completed the [round-robin tournament](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament"), with both teams winning six of their seven games. Canada only lost to the [Dordi Nordby](/wiki/Dordi_Nordby "Dordi Nordby") team from Norway, and Sweden's only loss was to Canada. In the semi-finals, the Canadian team defeated the team led by [Kirsty Hay](/wiki/Kirsty_Hay "Kirsty Hay") representing [team Great Britain](/wiki/Team_GB "Team GB") by a score of 6–5; and in the other semi-final, the team from [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") led by [Helena Blach Lavrsen](/wiki/Helena_Blach_Lavrsen "Helena Blach Lavrsen") defeated Sweden 7–5. In the gold medal game, Canada defeated Denmark by a score of 7–5. In the bronze medal game, Elisabet Gustafso's team from Sweden defeated Kirsty Hay's GB team by a score of 10–6.

### Ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ice hockey")]

Main article: [Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") matches took place at two purpose-built arenas in Nagano City, [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat") and [Aqua Wing Arena](/wiki/Aqua_Wing_Arena "Aqua Wing Arena"). The ice hockey events were significant for two reasons: the first Olympic ice hockey tournament for women and the participation of players from the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL"), which scheduled its first ever mid-season break. The [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") defeated [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") in the gold medal game for the men's title, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") defeated [Canada](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team") in the gold medal game for the women's title.

The men's competition began on 7 February with eight teams playing in two groups of four, Group A and B, with each team playing three games. The winners of these two groups, [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Kazakhstan men's national ice hockey team") and [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Belarus men's national ice hockey team"), advanced to join Groups C and D, composed of the six highest ranked men's national ice hockey teams in the world. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team"), and [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") were joined by Kazakhstan in Group C; [Canada](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada men's national ice hockey team"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Sweden men's national ice hockey team"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team") were joined by Belarus in Group D. On 22 February, with 10,010 spectators in attendance at [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat"), the Czech Republic defeated Russia in the gold medal game, 1–0, with the lone goal of the match scored with 12 minutes remaining. Finland defeated Canada for the bronze medal by a score of 3–2.

The first [women's ice hockey world championship](/wiki/IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship "IIHF Women's World Championship"), a biennial tournament, took place in [1990](/wiki/1990_IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship "1990 IIHF Women's World Championship"). Discussions to include women's ice hockey at the 1998 games began in 1992, and it was decided to include them in the program in 1993. The tournament included six teams playing in a one-group round-robin tournament. The top two team advanced to the gold medal game, and the teams ranked third and fourth played in the bronze medal match. The favorites were the [Canadians](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team"), who had won the three previous world championships, with the [Americans](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") finishing second each time. In the round-robin tournament, the Americans finished first, with the Canadians second. In the last round-robin game, the United States handily defeated Canada, 7–4, with the two teams scoring nine goals in the third period. In the gold medal match, with 8,626 fans in attendance at [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat"), the U.S. again defeated Canada, 3–1. Team [Finland](/wiki/Finland_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland women's national ice hockey team") defeated Team [China](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "China women's national ice hockey team") 4–1 for the bronze medal.

### Luge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Luge")]

Main article: [Luge at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Luge_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Luge at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Rodel-Weltcup-2005-Oberhof-Hackl.jpg/250px-Rodel-Weltcup-2005-Oberhof-Hackl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodel-Weltcup-2005-Oberhof-Hackl.jpg)

[Georg Hackl](/wiki/Georg_Hackl "Georg Hackl"), seen here during competition at [Oberhof, Germany](/wiki/Oberhof,_Germany "Oberhof, Germany") in 2005, won gold in the men's singles luge competition.

The [luge](/wiki/Luge "Luge") competitions took place in [Iizuna, Nagano](/wiki/Iizuna,_Nagano "Iizuna, Nagano"), at the [Spiral](/wiki/Spiral_(bobsleigh,_luge,_and_skeleton) "Spiral (bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton)") (Nagano Bobsleigh-Luge Park), the first purpose-built permanent bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton track in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"). In all, 24 nations took part in the luge events, with four countries, [India](/wiki/India_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "India at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "New Zealand at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Venezuela at the 1998 Winter Olympics") making their Olympic debut in luge events. There were three events, men's single, women's single, and doubles. [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won all three gold medals, one silver, and one bronze. The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won one silver and one bronze. [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") rounded out the medal table.

The first event with 24 lugers was the men's singles. Each athlete completed four runs over two days, 8 and 9 February. The [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") athlete, [Georg Hackl](/wiki/Georg_Hackl "Georg Hackl"), who had won gold at the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") and [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), had entered the competition winless in the 1997–1998 season. Hackl raced in a newly designed luge and aerodynamic shoes. Several team protested but these protests were rejected. Hackl dominated all four races, and finished with a time of 3:18.436, half a second ahead of the [Italian](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Armin Zöggeler](/wiki/Armin_Z%C3%B6ggeler "Armin Zöggeler"). Zöggeler finished .154 seconds ahead of [Jens Müller](/wiki/Jens_M%C3%BCller_(luger) "Jens Müller (luger)") of Germany, who had won gold at the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") when he competed for [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany").

On 10 and 11 February, the women's singles event took place, with each athlete completing four runs. In all, 29 athletes took part. The race for gold was very tight between two [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") athletes, [Silke Kraushaar](/wiki/Silke_Kraushaar "Silke Kraushaar") and [Barbara Niedernhuber](/wiki/Barbara_Niedernhuber "Barbara Niedernhuber"), with Kraushaar winning by .002 seconds, with a total time of 3:23.779 – the smallest margin of victory ever at the Olympics. [Angelika Neuner](/wiki/Angelika_Neuner "Angelika Neuner") of [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won the bronze, 0.474 seconds behind the gold medalist.

The two-race doubles competition, which in theory were open to females, consisted of 17 male pairs. The event took place 13 February. The [Germans](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Stefan Krauße](/wiki/Stefan_Krau%C3%9Fe "Stefan Krauße") and [Jan Behrendt](/wiki/Jan_Behrendt "Jan Behrendt"), who had competed together for 14 years, won the gold medal with a time of 1:41.105. Two [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") teams won silver and bronze, with [Chris Thorpe](/wiki/Chris_Thorpe "Chris Thorpe") and [Gordy Sheer](/wiki/Gordy_Sheer "Gordy Sheer") finishing .022 seconds behind the gold medalists and [Brian Martin](/wiki/Brian_Martin_(luger) "Brian Martin (luger)") and [Mark Grimmette](/wiki/Mark_Grimmette "Mark Grimmette") a further .09 seconds behind. The win by Krauße and Behrendt was their
four medal at the Olympics since they won silver at the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") when they competed for East Germany. This was the first time since the introduction of luge at the [1964 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1964_Winter_Olympics "1964 Winter Olympics") that athletes other than those from Austria, Germany, Italy, and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") won medals.

### Skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Skating")]

#### Figure skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Figure skating")]

Main article: [Figure skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") events took place at the [White Ring (arena)](/wiki/White_Ring_(arena) "White Ring (arena)"), an indoor arena built for the Games in Nagano City. Medals were awarded in four events: men's and women's singles, pair skating, and ice dance. The pairs event took place from 8–10 February, followed by the men's singles from 12 to 14 February, the ice dance from 13 to 16 February, and the women's singles from 18 to 20 February. The exhibition gala took place on 21 February. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won five medals, including three gold and two silver. The [USA](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won one gold and one silver. [France](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won two bronze medals. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won one silver, with [China](/wiki/China_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "China at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") each winning one bronze. [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") figure skater [Tara Lipinski](/wiki/Tara_Lipinski "Tara Lipinski") became the youngest competitor in Winter Olympics history to earn a gold medal in an individual event.[[19]](#cite_note-114)

#### Short track speed skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Short track speed skating")]

Main article: [Short track speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Short track speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

Six [short track speed skating](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating "Short track speed skating") events took place at the [White Ring (arena)](/wiki/White_Ring_(arena) "White Ring (arena)") from 17 to 21 February. A total of 18 nations were representing among the skaters. Four countries won medals. [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won six medals, including three gold. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won four medals, including two gold. Host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won one gold and one silver; and [China](/wiki/China_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "China at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won five silver and one bronze medal.

The 14th ranked [Japanese](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Takafumi Nishitani](/wiki/Takafumi_Nishitani "Takafumi Nishitani") beat the [Olympic record](/wiki/Olympic_record "Olympic record") in the 500m semi-finals. In the final, he led from the start and won the gold medal with a time of 42.862 seconds. The [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Marc Gagnon](/wiki/Marc_Gagnon "Marc Gagnon"), who was in second place, fell with two laps remaining. The [Chinese](/wiki/China_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "China at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [An Yulong](/wiki/An_Yulong "An Yulong") won the silver with a time of 43.022, 0.5 seconds of the Japanese skater [Hitoshi Uematsu](/wiki/Hitoshi_Uematsu "Hitoshi Uematsu"). In the 1000 meters, world record holder Marc Gagnon was disqualified for obstruction in the quarter-finals. The Chinese skater [Li Jiajun](/wiki/Li_Jiajun "Li Jiajun"), who led for most of the final, was passed by the [South Korean](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater, [Kim Dong-sung](/wiki/Kim_Dong-sung "Kim Dong-sung"), in the final corner. Kim won with a time of 1:32.375, 0.053 seconds ahead of the silver medalist. The Canadian [Éric Bédard](/wiki/%C3%89ric_B%C3%A9dard "Éric Bédard") won the bronze, .223 seconds further behind. In the 5000m relays, world-title holders from [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") led at the beginning of the relay but were passed by the Canadians, and fell. With about one-quarter of the race left, a Chinese skater fell, bringing down with him a South Korean skater, allowing the Canadians to easily win the gold, with a time of 7:06.075. The South Koreans were .701 seconds behind, with the Chinese finishing with the bronze a further 4 seconds back. The Japanese team won the B-Final with a time that was five seconds faster than the gold medalists.

In the women's 500m final, the [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Isabelle Charest](/wiki/Isabelle_Charest "Isabelle Charest") collided with the [Chinese](/wiki/China_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "China at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Wang Chunlu](/wiki/Wang_Chunlu "Wang Chunlu"), and both fell. Charest was disqualified and Wang, angry, never finished the race. The Canadian [Annie Perreault](/wiki/Annie_Perreault "Annie Perreault") won the gold with a time of 46.568 seconds, 0.059 seconds ahead of [Yang Yang (S)](/wiki/Yang_Yang_(S) "Yang Yang (S)") of China. Because these were the only two to finish the race, the bronze medal went to the winner of the B-Final, the [South Korean](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Chun Lee-kyung](/wiki/Chun_Lee-kyung "Chun Lee-kyung"). In the 1000m race, the Chinese skater [Yang Yang (A)](/wiki/Yang_Yang_(A) "Yang Yang (A)") led the race but was passed by the 500m bronze medalist, Chun, in the last straight away to the finish line. Chun won the race with a time of 1:42.776 seconds. Yang Yang (A) was disqualified for using her elbow to try to block Chun. Yang Yang (S) won the silver, 0.567 seconds behind the gold medalist from South Korea. [Won Hye-kyung](/wiki/Won_Hye-kyung "Won Hye-kyung"), also of South Korea, won the bronze a further 0.18 seconds behind. In the 3000m relay, the Chinese team led for most of the race but the South Korean skater [Kim Yun-mi](/wiki/Kim_Yun-mi_(speed_skater) "Kim Yun-mi (speed skater)") passed Yang Yang (A) in the last changeover. Both teams beat the [World Record](/wiki/World_Record "World Record"), with the South Koreans finishing with a time of 4:16.260, and the Chinese were 0.123 seconds behind. The Canadian team won bronze with a time of 4:21.205.

#### Speed skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Speed skating")]

Main article: [Speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Marianne_Timmer_%2809-12-2007%29.jpg/250px-Marianne_Timmer_%2809-12-2007%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marianne_Timmer_(09-12-2007).jpg)

[Marianne Timmer](/wiki/Marianne_Timmer "Marianne Timmer") won two gold medals for the Netherlands in speed skating.

From 8–20 February 171 athletes from 25 countries took part in the long-track [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating "Speed skating") events that were held in Nagano City at [M-Wave](/wiki/M-Wave "M-Wave"), Japan's first indoor, long-track speed skating venue. In all, eight countries won medals. The [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Netherlands at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won 11 medals, including five gold and four silver. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and the [USA](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") also won multiple medals. Twelve [Olympic records](/wiki/Olympic_records "Olympic records") and five [World records](/wiki/World_records "World records") were established at the Games on the ice at M-Wave. [Gianni Romme](/wiki/Gianni_Romme "Gianni Romme") and [Marianne Timmer](/wiki/Marianne_Timmer "Marianne Timmer"), both of the Netherlands, each won two gold medals. The Nagano Olympics were the first where athletes wore [clap skates](/wiki/Clap_skate "Clap skate").

On the men's side, the world record holder in the men's 500m was the [Japanese](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Hiroyasu Shimizu](/wiki/Hiroyasu_Shimizu "Hiroyasu Shimizu"). Shimizu was the smallest skater at the Games, 1.62m tall. The 500m was run over two races for the first time at these Games. Shimizu was fastest in both races becoming only the second ever Japanese to win a singles title at the Olympic Games. Finishing in second and third were the [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skaters, [Jeremy Wotherspoon](/wiki/Jeremy_Wotherspoon "Jeremy Wotherspoon") and [Kevin Overland](/wiki/Kevin_Overland "Kevin Overland"), who are 1.91m and 1.84 m tall, respectively. Shimizu's combined time was 1:11.35, 0.49 seconds ahead of Wotherspoon, and another 0.02 seconds ahead of Overland. The 1500m was won by [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Ådne Søndrål](/wiki/%C3%85dne_S%C3%B8ndr%C3%A5l "Ådne Søndrål") with a [world record](/wiki/World_record "World record") time of 1:47.87. Søndrål was 0.26 and 0.65 seconds ahead of two [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Netherlands at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skaters, [Ids Postma](/wiki/Ids_Postma "Ids Postma"), and [Rintje Ritsma](/wiki/Rintje_Ritsma "Rintje Ritsma"). In the 1000m, Postma won gold, with a time of 1:10.64, followed by another Dutch skater [Jan Bos](/wiki/Jan_Bos "Jan Bos"), who was 0.07 seconds behind, and Shimizu who won the bronze with a time of 1:11.00. In the 5000m, the Dutch skater [Gianni Romme](/wiki/Gianni_Romme "Gianni Romme") won gold, with a world record time of 6:22.20, followed by Ritsma, who was 6.04 seconds behind, and Bart Veldkamp, representing [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Belgium at the 1998 Winter Olympics") who won the bronze with a time of 6:28.31. Finally, in the 10,000m, three Dutch skaters won medals. Romme won gold with a world record time, 15 seconds ahead of the world record, of 13:15.33, [Bob de Jong](/wiki/Bob_de_Jong "Bob de Jong") won silver, and Ritsma won bronze.

On the women's side, the 500 m title was won by the Canadian [Catriona Le May Doan](/wiki/Catriona_Le_May_Doan "Catriona Le May Doan"), the favorite, who beat or equalled the world record four times before the Games. Her teammate, [Susan Auch](/wiki/Susan_Auch "Susan Auch"), finished second. Both were coached by Susan's brother, Derrick Auch. [Tomomi Okazaki](/wiki/Tomomi_Okazaki "Tomomi Okazaki"), of host Japan, won the bronze medal. In the 1500m, [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Netherlands at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Marianne Timmer](/wiki/Marianne_Timmer "Marianne Timmer") won gold with a world record time of 1:57.58. The [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Gunda Niemann](/wiki/Gunda_Niemann-Stirnemann "Gunda Niemann-Stirnemann") was second, 1.08 seconds behind, and the [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Chris Witty](/wiki/Chris_Witty "Chris Witty") won bronze with a time of 1:58.97. In the 1000m, Timmer won gold again, with a time of 1:16.51. Witty won silver, 0.26 seconds behind, and Le May Doan won bronze with a time of 1:17.37. The [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Franziska Schenk](/wiki/Franziska_Schenk "Franziska Schenk"), one of the favorites, fell during the second lap. In the 3000m, German skaters won all three medals. Niemann won gold with a time of 4:07.29; Claudia Pechstein won silver, 1.18 seconds back; and [Anni Friesinger](/wiki/Anni_Friesinger-Postma "Anni Friesinger-Postma") won bronze with a time of 4:09.44. Finally, in the 5000m, Pechstein won gold with a world record time of 6:59.61; Niemann was 0.04 seconds back for silver, and the [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakhstan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Kazakhstan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skater [Lyudmila Prokasheva](/wiki/Lyudmila_Prokasheva "Lyudmila Prokasheva") won bronze, with a time of 7:11.14. Prokasheva's medal was the first medal by a female Kazakh athlete at any Winter Olympics.

### Skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Skiing")]

#### Alpine skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Alpine skiing")]

Main article: [Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [Alpine](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skiing events took place at Hakuba Happoone Winter Resort in [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba,_Nagano "Hakuba, Nagano") village, 50 kilometers west of Nagano City, and at [Mount Higashidate](/wiki/Mount_Higashidate "Mount Higashidate") in the [Shiga Highlands](/wiki/Shiga_Highlands "Shiga Highlands") in [Yamanouchi, Nagano](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"), 30 kilometers northeast of Nagano City. In all, 249 athletes, 141 males and 108 females, from 49 countries, took part in the 10 Alpine skiing events, men's and women's downhill, super-G, giant slalom, slalom, and combined. [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won 11 medals, including three gold. [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") also won three gold, and six medals in total. Seven other countries also won medals, including [Australia](/wiki/Australia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Australia at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), whose [Zali Steggall](/wiki/Zali_Steggall "Zali Steggall") won that countries first ever individual [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") medal. The most successful athletes at these Games were [Katja Seizinger](/wiki/Katja_Seizinger "Katja Seizinger") from Germany, who won two gold medals and one bronze; and [Hermann Maier](/wiki/Hermann_Maier "Hermann Maier"), from Austria, who won two gold medals.

#### Cross-country skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Cross-country skiing")]

Main article: [Cross-country skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie_2011-01-26_001_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie_2011-01-26_001_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie_2011-01-26_001_(cropped).jpg)

[Bjørn Dæhlie](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie "Bjørn Dæhlie"), pictured in January 2011

The [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)") events took place at [Nozawa Onsen Ski Resort](/wiki/Nozawa_Onsen_Ski_Resort "Nozawa Onsen Ski Resort"), in the town of [Nozawaonsen](/wiki/Nozawaonsen,_Nagano "Nozawaonsen, Nagano"), approximately 50 kilometers north of Nagano. In all, 228 athletes, including 126 men and 102 women, from 37 countries took part. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won eight medals, including all the women five gold medals, and [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won nine medals, including four gold medals. Six other countries also won medals, including [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") with one gold and two bronze, and [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") with two silver and two bronze. [Larisa Lazutina](/wiki/Larisa_Lazutina "Larisa Lazutina") from Russia won five medals, including three gold; and [Bjørn Dæhlie](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie "Bjørn Dæhlie") from Norway won four medals, including three gold.

#### Freestyle skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Freestyle skiing")]

Main article: [Freestyle skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [freestyle skiing](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing "Freestyle skiing") competition was held at the [Iizuna Kogen Ski Area](/wiki/Iizuna_Kogen_Ski_Area "Iizuna Kogen Ski Area"), 12 kilometers north of Nagano, from 8 to 18 February. It was the third consecutive Games that freestyle skiing events took place. The four events, men's and women's moguls and aerials, involved 110 athletes from 25 countries . The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won three gold medals. Host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won one gold medal. Athletes from [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won a silver and a bronze medal. Six other countries took home either one silver or one bronze medal.

In men's moguls, the [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Jonny Moseley](/wiki/Jonny_Moseley "Jonny Moseley") was first after the qualifications. Two cousins from [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Janne Lahtela](/wiki/Janne_Lahtela "Janne Lahtela") and [Sami Mustonen](/wiki/Sami_Mustonen "Sami Mustonen"), who had never medalled at the [FIS Freestyle Ski World Cup](/wiki/FIS_Freestyle_Ski_World_Cup "FIS Freestyle Ski World Cup"), were ranked second and third behind Moseley. Moseley easily won the final with a score of 26.93. Lahtela was .93 points behind, and Mustonen was another .24 points behind. The [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Jean-Luc Brassard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Brassard "Jean-Luc Brassard"), gold medalist from the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), finished in fourth. In men's aerials, the American [Eric Bergoust](/wiki/Eric_Bergoust "Eric Bergoust"), who had fallen during training, overtook the other competitors with a score of 255.64 points. The [Frenchmen](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Sébastien Foucras](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Foucras "Sébastien Foucras"), and the [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Dmitri Dashinski](/wiki/Dmitri_Dashinski "Dmitri Dashinski"), were second and third. The Canadian, [Nicolas Fontaine](/wiki/Nicolas_Fontaine_(skier) "Nicolas Fontaine (skier)"), [world champion](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_FIS_Freestyle_Skiing_World_Cup "1996–97 FIS Freestyle Skiing World Cup") in 1997, only managed 10th place after falling on his second jump.

The [Japanese](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") moguls skier, [Tae Satoya](/wiki/Tae_Satoya "Tae Satoya"), 11th after qualifications, surprised everyone by winning the gold medal with a score of 25.06. She was the first female Japanese Olympic champion. The [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Tatjana Mittermayer](/wiki/Tatjana_Mittermayer "Tatjana Mittermayer") scored 24.62 points and won the silver medal. The [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Kari Traa](/wiki/Kari_Traa "Kari Traa"), won the bronze with a score of 24.09 points. In women's aerials, [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Nikki Stone](/wiki/Nikki_Stone "Nikki Stone") won the gold medal with a score of 193.00 points. The ex-gymnast, [Xu Nannan](/wiki/Xu_Nannan "Xu Nannan") from [China](/wiki/China_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "China at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won silver with a score of 186.97, and [Colette Brand](/wiki/Colette_Brand "Colette Brand") from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won bronze with a score of 171.83.

#### Nordic combined skiing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Nordic combined skiing")]

Main article: [Nordic combined at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Nordic combined at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [Nordic combined](/wiki/Nordic_combined "Nordic combined") events were held at the [Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium](/wiki/Hakuba_Ski_Jumping_Stadium "Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium") and the [Snow Harp](/wiki/Snow_Harp "Snow Harp"), both in [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba,_Nagano "Hakuba, Nagano") village, 50 kilometers west of Nagano City. In all, 53 athletes from 14 countries, took part in the two events, individual and team. [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won both gold medals. [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won both silver medals. [France](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") each won one of the bronze medals.

The first event was the individual competition that took place on 13 and 14 February. In all, there were 48 athletes. The silver medalist from the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Bjarte Engen Vik](/wiki/Bjarte_Engen_Vik "Bjarte Engen Vik"), was the [1997–98 FIS Nordic Combined World Cup](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_FIS_Nordic_Combined_World_Cup "1997–98 FIS Nordic Combined World Cup") leader. At the Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium, Vik led after the first two jumps. He was followed by the [Russian](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Valeri Stoliarov](/wiki/Valeri_Stoliarov "Valeri Stoliarov"). The following day, the skiers left, in order of the placement following the ski jump, along te 15 kilometer cross-country race at the Snow Harp. The race was skied in the rain. Vik led throughout and finished with a 27.5 second lead over second place. With three kilometers to the finish line, the [Finnish](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") athlete, 18-year-old [Samppa Lajunen](/wiki/Samppa_Lajunen "Samppa Lajunen"), who was sixth after the jumps, caught up with Stoliarov. The skied together until the stadium, and 60 meters from the finish line, Lajunen passed the Russian and picked up the silver medal 0.7 seconds ahead of Stoliarov who won the bronze. The fastest athlete on the course was the [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") skier, [Marco Zarucchi](/wiki/Marco_Zarucchi "Marco Zarucchi"), who was 43rd after the jumps, finished in 25th place.

Eleven nations took part in the team event on 19 and 20 February. At previous Olympics, the team event involved three athletes per team, with the completing a 3x10 kilometer relay. At Nagano, the team was enlarged to four athletes who completed a 4x5 kilometer relay. After the jumps, the team from Finland led by four seconds ahead of the [Austrians](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), eight seconds ahead of the Norwegians, nine ahead of the [Czechs](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and 29 seconds ahead of the [Japanese](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics"). The relay took place in rain. The Norwegians quickly took the lead and never looked back. The last Norwegian skier had the time to grab his country's flag with 500 meters from the finish line, and they won gold with more than one minute lead over the team from Finland. The [French](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics") team, sixth after the jumps, won the bronze medal ahead of the Austrians. The Japanese, gold medalists at the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") and [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics") finished in fifth.

#### Ski jumping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Ski jumping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/FunakiKazuyoshiOkuryama2014.jpg/250px-FunakiKazuyoshiOkuryama2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:FunakiKazuyoshiOkuryama2014.jpg)

[Kazuyoshi Funaki](/wiki/Kazuyoshi_Funaki "Kazuyoshi Funaki") (pictured in 2014) won two gold medals and one silver for host Japan.

Main article: [Ski jumping at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ski jumping at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

The [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping") competitions took place at the [Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium](/wiki/Hakuba_Ski_Jumping_Stadium "Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium") in [Hakuba](/wiki/Hakuba,_Nagano "Hakuba, Nagano") village, 50 kilometers west of Nagano City. In all, 68 athletes from 19 countries participated. For the first time, the top 30 jumpers qualified for the second round. Host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won the most medals, including two gold in the large hill and large hill team. [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") rounded out the medal table. [Kazuyoshi Funaki](/wiki/Kazuyoshi_Funaki "Kazuyoshi Funaki") from [Yoichi, Hokkaido](/wiki/Yoichi,_Hokkaido "Yoichi, Hokkaido") in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") won two gold and one silver for the host country.

The normal hill jumps took place on 11 February in front of 45,000 spectators. The Japanese, who had dominated the [1997–98 FIS Ski Jumping World Cup](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_FIS_Ski_Jumping_World_Cup "1997–98 FIS Ski Jumping World Cup") season, were the favorites. With a jump of 91.5 meters, [Masahiko Harada](/wiki/Masahiko_Harada "Masahiko Harada") led after the first round ahead of the Finnish jumper, [Jani Soininen](/wiki/Jani_Soininen "Jani Soininen")
[Kazuyoshi Funaki](/wiki/Kazuyoshi_Funaki "Kazuyoshi Funaki"), who was fourth after the first round, took the lead with a jump of 90.5 meters in the second round. After a delay caused by strong wind, Soininen took the lead with only Harada still to jump. A sidewind blew when Harada jumped, and only managed 84.5 meters to finish in fifth place overall. Soininen won gold with 234.5 points, Funaki was second with 233.5, and the Austrian [Andreas Widhölzl](/wiki/Andreas_Widh%C3%B6lzl "Andreas Widhölzl") finished third with 232.5 points.

On 15 February, the large hill jump competition took place. 60,000 spectators gathered at Hakuba Ski Jumping Stadium. Normal hill bronze medalist Widhölzl led after the first round, ahead of the Japanese jumper [Takanobu Okabe](/wiki/Takanobu_Okabe "Takanobu Okabe"), Jani Soininen et Funaki. In the second round, Funaki jumped 132.5 m, and, for the first time at the Olympics, received perfect points for his style. He jumped into first place and won the gold medal with 272.3 points overall. It was the first Japanese gold medal in ski jumping since the [1972 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics") in [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"). Harada jumped next. Unfortunately, the measurement system was installed between 95 and 135 meters and his jump was beyond that. He was measured manually to be 136 meters. He also had good points but only managed to win the bronze medal with 258.3. meters. Soininen won the silver with a combined score of 260.8 points.

At the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), the Japanese team were the favorites but Harada jumped poorly, costing the Japanese the gold medal. Again, in 1998, the Japanese were the favorites. The team event took place on 17 February. The start was slowed by 30 minutes because of heavy falling snow. The first two Japanese jumpers, Okabe at [Hiroya Saitō](/wiki/Hiroya_Sait%C5%8D "Hiroya Saitō"), jumped Japan into first place. Harada completely missed his jump, jumping only 79.5 meters, and despite Funaki having a good jump, Japan drop from first to fourth after the first round behind Austria, Germany, and Norway. Okabe jumped 137 meters, which was an Olympic record. Saitō followed this with a good jump. Harada was next, and like Okabe, jumped 137 meters. The last jumper was Funaki who jumped 125 meters, and the Japanese team became Olympic champions with 933.0 points. The Germans won silver with 897.4 points, and the Austrians finished with 881.5 points for the bronze.

#### Snowboarding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Snowboarding")]

Main article: [Snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Rachael_Ashe_Photo_-_Coffee_with_Ross_Rebagliati_-_8.JPG/250px-Rachael_Ashe_Photo_-_Coffee_with_Ross_Rebagliati_-_8.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rachael_Ashe_Photo_-_Coffee_with_Ross_Rebagliati_-_8.JPG)

[Ross Rebagliati](/wiki/Ross_Rebagliati "Ross Rebagliati") (pictured in 2007) won the first-ever gold medal in men's giant slalom, before being disqualified, and then having his medal reinstated.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Nicola_Thost.JPG/250px-Nicola_Thost.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nicola_Thost.JPG)

[Nicola Thost](/wiki/Nicola_Thost "Nicola Thost") (pictured in 2015) won the gold medal in women's halfpipe.

In the decade leading up the games, snowboarding had become popular in both North America and Europe, as well as Japan, and as a result, in August 1994, the [NAOC](/wiki/Nagano_Olympic_Organizing_Committee "Nagano Olympic Organizing Committee") received a request from the IOC president Samaranch to consider including snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics.[[a 60]](#cite_note-115) To reduce costs, NAOC asked the host community to cover a portion of the costs – the town Yamanouchi agreed – and [FIS](/wiki/International_Ski_Federation "International Ski Federation") was expected to support financially as well.[[a 61]](#cite_note-116) In November 1995, the NAOC executive board agreed to add snowboarding, and this was approved by the IOC at their December meeting the following month in [Karuizawa](/wiki/Karuizawa "Karuizawa").[[a 62]](#cite_note-117) This was the first Winter Olympics with [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding") events. The events took place at [Mount Yakebitai](/wiki/Mount_Yakebitai "Mount Yakebitai") and [Kanbayashi Snowboard Park](/wiki/Kanbayashi_Snowboard_Park "Kanbayashi Snowboard Park") in [Yamanouchi, Nagano](/wiki/Yamanouchi,_Nagano "Yamanouchi, Nagano"), 30 kilometers northeast of Nagano City, from 8 to 12 February. In all, 125 athletes from 22 countries participated in the men's and women's halfpipe and giant slalom. Athletes from [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won two medals, including one gold. Athletes from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [France](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") also won gold medals.

In the men's giant slalom, the [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Jasey-Jay Anderson](/wiki/Jasey-Jay_Anderson "Jasey-Jay Anderson") won the first race with a half-second lead ahead of Rebagliati. During the second race, the event was temporary delayed because of snow and fog. [Ross Rebagliati](/wiki/Ross_Rebagliati "Ross Rebagliati") finished with a combined time of 2:03.96, 0.02 seconds ahead of the [Italian](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Thomas Prugger](/wiki/Thomas_Prugger "Thomas Prugger"), and another 0.10 seconds ahead of the [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Ueli Kestenholz](/wiki/Ueli_Kestenholz "Ueli Kestenholz"). Controversy occurred when three days after the men's giant slalom, the International Olympic Committee determined that gold medalist Rebagliati from Canada, was disqualified after testing positive for [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana").[[b 36]](#cite_note-118) It was the first time in Olympic history that an athlete was disqualified for marijuana. The [Canadian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Canadian_Olympic_Committee "Canadian Olympic Committee") lodged a protest and the case quickly went to the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") where it was ruled that because marijuana was not classified as a "banned" substance, the medal should be returned to the Canadian athlete. In the halfpipe, the gold medal went to the [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Gian Simmen](/wiki/Gian_Simmen "Gian Simmen"), who had the highest score, 85.2, despite a heavy rain. The [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Daniel Franck](/wiki/Daniel_Franck "Daniel Franck") won the silver with a score of 82.4, and the [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Ross Powers](/wiki/Ross_Powers "Ross Powers") won the bronze with a score of 82.1.

The women's giant slalom was delayed one day because of a snowstorm. The big favorite, the [Frenchwoman](/wiki/France_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Karine Ruby](/wiki/Karine_Ruby "Karine Ruby") won the first race with almost two seconds ahead of her compatriot [Isabelle Blanc](/wiki/Isabelle_Blanc "Isabelle Blanc"). Ruby won the second race, with Blanc missing the last gate and falling. Ruby's combined time was 2:17.34. The [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Heidi Renoth](/wiki/Heidi_Renoth "Heidi Renoth") won the silver with a time of 2:19.17, and the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Brigitte Köck](/wiki/Brigitte_K%C3%B6ck "Brigitte Köck") won the bronze with a time of 2:19.42. In the halfpipe, the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Stine Brun Kjeldaas](/wiki/Stine_Brun_Kjeldaas "Stine Brun Kjeldaas") won the qualification round. However, in the finals, the [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Nicola Thost](/wiki/Nicola_Thost "Nicola Thost"), a former gymnast, finished second in both legs, scored 74.6 points, which was enough for the gold medal. Stine Brun Kjeldaas finished fourth in the first leg and first in second, winning the silver with 74.2 points. The [American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Shannon Dunn-Downing](/wiki/Shannon_Dunn-Downing "Shannon Dunn-Downing") won the first leg, but finished seventh in the second leg, leaving her with the bronze with a score of 72.8.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1998_Winter_Olympics&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Wakasato_Municipal_Cultural_Hall.jpg/250px-Wakasato_Municipal_Cultural_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wakasato_Municipal_Cultural_Hall.jpg)

Wakasato Civic Cultural Center in June 2006

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/K%27sTown_Wakasato_Nagano_Japan.jpg/250px-K%27sTown_Wakasato_Nagano_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:K%27sTown_Wakasato_Nagano_Japan.jpg)

The former IBC was converted into a department store

The Nagano Olympics were covered by more than 10,000 members of the media, including 8,329 accredited journalists, of which 2,586 were from newspaper media and 5,743 television and radio journalists. The Organizing Committee established a Main Press Center (MPC), over two buildings, and 17 annexes throughout the different sites. The [International Broadcast Centre](/wiki/International_Broadcast_Centre "International Broadcast Centre") (IBC) was a temporary one-storey facility constructed on the grounds of a former textile mill in the immediate vicinity of the MPC and Big Hat.[[a 63]](#cite_note-119) A wide range of general services, such as a restaurants, cafeterias, banks, post offices and a medical clinics were available at the IBC.[[a 64]](#cite_note-120) The IBC was later converted into a departament store, *K's Town Wakasato Store*.

The MBC, which is today the *Wakasato Civic Cultural Center*,[[20]](#cite_note-121) was built beside [Big Hat](/wiki/Big_Hat "Big Hat"), the main [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics") venue. The MBC had a surface area of 42,728 m2, with one principal room for 600 journalists of 1430m2 and another of 5100m2 that was rented by various press agencies.[[a 65]](#cite_note-122) The largest press offices at the Games were [Kyodo News](/wiki/Kyodo_News "Kyodo News"), [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), [Agence France-Presse](/wiki/Agence_France-Presse "Agence France-Presse"), [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), and [Deutsche Presse-Agentur](/wiki/Deutsche_Presse-Agentur "Deutsche Presse-Agentur").[[a 66]](#cite_note-123) The MBC also included a press conference room for 600 people.[[a 67]](#cite_note-124)

The host broadcaster for the Games, the Olympic Radio and Televisions Organization (ORTO'98) was established as a separate organization within NAOC, the organizing committee.[[a 68]](#cite_note-125) ORTO'98 was joint-venture created between [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK"), the Japanese public broadcaster, the National Association of Broadcasters (NAB), and NAOC.[[a 69]](#cite_note-126) A total of 1647 staff worked 386 cameras at the various venues and events,[[a 70]](#cite_note-127) with coverage increasing by 55% over the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics") in [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer").[[a 71]](#cite_note-128) The Games were broadcast in 160 countries, 40 more than in Lillehammer,[[a 72]](#cite_note-129) and it was estimated that 10.7 billion viewers watched the Games over the 16-day period.[[a 73]](#cite_note-130) Some events were filmed and broadcast in [analog high-definition television](/wiki/Multiple_sub-Nyquist_sampling_encoding "Multiple sub-Nyquist sampling encoding"), with NHK broadcasting over 270 hours of coverage in Japan.[[21]](#cite_note-131)

Broadcasting rights totaled USD$513 million, which was a record for the Winter Olympics, and all contracts with 16 broadcasting rights' holders were record sums.[[a 74]](#cite_note-132) This money was split 60–40 between NAOC and the International Olympic Committee.[[a 75]](#cite_note-133) The [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") broadcasting network, [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), paid 375 million US dollars, to air the Games in the United States. The 1998 Winter Olympics are the most recent Olympic Games to not air on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") in the United States, as the current contract with [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") started in 1999.
